---
title: "Nonparametric Tests: When the Assumptions Break Down"
subtitle: "Not covered in textbook"
author: "Emile Latour, Nicky Wakim, Meike Niederhausen"
date: "`r library(here); source(here('class_dates.R')); w10d1`"
date-format: long
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: "../../assets/css/reveal-bmsc620_v5.scss"
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    width: 1955
    height: 1100
    footer: "BMSC 620 | Nonparametric Tests"
    html-math-method: mathjax
    chalkboard: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(glue)
library(here)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(oibiostat)
library(rstatix)
library(janitor)
library(gt)

library(lamisc)
library(laviz)

# Set theme for plots
theme_set(laviz::theme_minimal_white(grid = "none",
                                     axis = "xy",
                                     base_size = 16))

set.seed(620)
```

# Learning Objectives

By the end of today's lecture, you will be able to:

1. Explain when and why nonparametric tests are preferred over parametric alternatives
2. Describe the core idea behind rank-based tests
3. Apply the Wilcoxon signed-rank test to paired or single-sample data
4. Apply the Wilcoxon rank-sum test to two independent samples
5. Apply the Kruskal-Wallis test to compare three or more groups
6. Choose the appropriate nonparametric test for a given study design

## Roadmap for Today

\

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Part 1: Parametric vs. Nonparametric**

- When do the assumptions break down?
- The rank-based approach
- What we gain and what we lose
- The complete comparison table

**Part 2: Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test**

- The sign test (brief)
- Wilcoxon signed-rank: the upgrade
- Glaucoma example (paired data)
- Running it in R
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Part 3: Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test**

- The nonparametric two-sample t-test
- Rat neuron example
- Running it in R

**Part 4: Kruskal-Wallis Test**

- The nonparametric ANOVA
- IL-6 example
- Running it in R
- Post-hoc follow-up
:::
:::::

# Part 1: Parametric vs. Nonparametric

## What do all our tests so far have in common?

\

Every test we've learned assumes the data come from a specific distribution:

\

| Test | Distributional assumption |
|---|---|
| One-sample t-test | Normal population (or large $n$) |
| Paired t-test | Normal differences (or large $n$) |
| Two-sample t-test | Normal populations (or large $n$) |
| ANOVA | Normal within each group (or large $n$) |

\

**These are called *parametric* tests** — they assume the population can be described by specific parameters ($\mu$, $\sigma$) under a named distribution.

\

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### What if the assumptions don't hold?

Small samples + non-normal data + outliers = parametric tests may not be valid. Nonparametric tests are the answer.
:::

## When do parametric assumptions break down?

\

::::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

**Situation 1: Small samples with skewed data**

- $n = 8$ per group, right-skewed outcome
- Central limit theorem doesn't save you here
- t-test p-values may not be reliable

\

**Situation 2: Outliers that aren't errors**

- Biological data often has extreme values
- A single outlier can dominate the mean
- The outlier is real — you can't just remove it

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

**Situation 3: Ordinal outcomes**

- Pain scale (1–10), severity scores, Likert items
- Means aren't always meaningful
- "Average pain of 4.7" — what does that mean?

\

**Situation 4: Heavy-tailed distributions**

- Gene expression, antibody titers, viral loads
- Extreme values are common, not aberrant
- Log-transformation helps, but isn't always appropriate

:::
:::::

## The rank-based approach

**Key idea:** Instead of testing raw values, convert them to ranks first.

\

::::: columns
::: {.column width="15%"}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}

**Step 1:** Take your original data

| Patient | Viral load |
|---|---|
| A | 125 |
| B | 3024 |
| C | 780 |
| D | 55 |
| E | 426 |

:::

::: {.column width="0%"}
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}

**Step 2:** Replace values with ranks

| Patient | Viral load | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| D | 55 | 1 |
| A | 125 | 2 |
| E | 426 | 3 |
| C | 780 | 4 |
| B | 3024 | 5 |

:::
:::::

\

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
### Why ranks work

Patient B's viral load of 3024 is extreme, but as a rank it's just "5" — the highest. Ranks compress outliers automatically. The rank-sum test uses these ranks instead of raw values, so no distributional assumption is needed.
:::

## Pros and cons of nonparametric tests

::::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### Advantages

- **Fewer assumptions** — no normality required
- **Robust to outliers** — extreme values become just another rank
- **Work with small samples** — exact p-values available
- **Can handle ordinal data** — ranks are meaningful for ordered categories
:::

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
### Trade-offs

- **Less powerful** — converting to ranks discards information about magnitude
- If parametric assumptions *are* met, the t-test or ANOVA will detect effects more efficiently
- Confidence intervals are harder to compute
- Ties in ranks require corrections
:::

:::
:::::

\

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
### The bottom line

Use parametric tests when assumptions are met — they're more powerful. Switch to nonparametric when you have small samples, severe skew, heavy outliers, or ordinal data.
:::

## The complete comparison table

\

| Study design | Parametric test | Nonparametric alternative |
|---|---|---|
| Single sample, numeric | One-sample t-test | Wilcoxon signed-rank |
| Paired sample, numeric | Paired t-test | Wilcoxon signed-rank |
| Two independent samples, numeric | Two-sample t-test | Wilcoxon rank-sum |
| 3+ independent samples, numeric | ANOVA | Kruskal-Wallis |

\

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### What about categorical outcomes?

For 2×2 tables with small expected cell counts, we use **Fisher's exact test** — we covered this already in Lesson 14. It's the nonparametric alternative to the chi-squared test for categorical data.
:::

# Part 2: Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test

## First: the sign test (brief)

\

<!-- The **sign test** is the simplest nonparametric test for paired or single-sample data: -->

<!-- - Compute the differences $d_i = x_{i2} - x_{i1}$ -->
<!-- - Count the number of **positive** signs ($D^+$) and **negative** signs ($D^-$) -->
<!-- - Under $H_0$: differences symmetric around 0, so $D^+ \sim \text{Binomial}(n, 0.5)$ -->

The **sign test** is the simplest nonparametric test for paired or single-sample data.

\

**Paired setting:** each subject $i$ has two measurements — a first observation
$x_{i1}$ (e.g., pre-treatment) and a second observation $x_{i2}$ (e.g., post-treatment).
Compute the within-subject difference:

$$d_i = x_{i2} - x_{i1}$$

**Single-sample setting:** each subject has one observation $x_i$, and we compare
it against a hypothesized value $\mu_0$:

$$d_i = x_i - \mu_0$$

\

In both cases, the test proceeds the same way:

- Count the number of **positive** differences ($D^+$) and **negative** differences ($D^-$)
- Ignore ties ($d_i = 0$)
- Under $H_0$: differences are symmetric around 0, so $D^+ \sim \text{Binomial}(n, 0.5)$

\

## Why we don't use the sign test

\

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
### The problem with the sign test

The sign test only uses the **direction** of each difference — not the **size**.

A difference of 0.01 and a difference of 10.0 both count as a single "+". This
throws away most of the information in your data and makes the test much less
powerful than it could be.

**The Wilcoxon signed-rank test solves this by incorporating the magnitude of
differences through ranking.**
:::

## The Wilcoxon signed-rank test

\

**The upgrade over the sign test:** We rank the *absolute values* of the differences, then use the signs.

\

**Conceptual steps:**

1. Compute differences $d_i$ (drop any zeros)
2. Rank the **absolute values** $|d_i|$ from smallest to largest (ties get average ranks)
3. Attach the original sign (+/−) back to each rank
4. Sum the positive ranks ($T^+$) and negative ranks ($T^-$)
5. If $H_0$ is true, $T^+$ and $T^-$ should be roughly equal

\

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### What's being tested

$H_0$: The population of differences is **symmetric around zero**

$H_A$: The population of differences is **not symmetric around zero**

This is equivalent to testing whether the median difference is zero — provided the distribution is symmetric.
:::

## The glaucoma example

Intraocular pressure in glaucoma patients is often reduced by treatment with adrenaline. A **new synthetic drug** is being evaluated — more expensive, but potentially more effective.

- 7 patients treated with **both** drugs (one eye each)
- Outcome: reduction in pressure after treatment
- Larger values = greater pressure reduction

**Question:** Do the two drugs produce the same reduction in pressure?

\

```{r}
#| echo: false

IOP <- tibble(
  patient = 1:7,
  adrenaline = c(3.5, 2.6, 3.0, 1.9, 2.9, 2.4, 2.0),
  synthetic  = c(3.2, 3.1, 3.3, 2.4, 2.9, 2.8, 2.6)
) %>%
  mutate(d = synthetic - adrenaline)

```

```{r}
IOP
```


## Step 1: State hypotheses

\

$H_0$: The differences in intraocular pressure reduction (synthetic − adrenaline) are symmetrically distributed around zero

$H_A$: The differences are not symmetrically distributed around zero

\

In plain language:

$H_0$: The two drugs produce the same median reduction in intraocular pressure

$H_A$: The two drugs differ in median reduction of intraocular pressure

## Step 2: Set significance level

$$\alpha = 0.05$$

## Step 3: Check assumptions

\

**Wilcoxon signed-rank assumptions:**

1. Paired observations (✓ — same patients, one eye each)
2. Differences are **continuous** (✓ — pressure measurements)
3. Distribution of differences is **symmetric** around the median (check visually)

\

- Assumptions 1 and 2 are verified from the study design alone.
- For assumption 3, we plot the distribution of differences $d_i$ and look for approximate symmetry around zero — no need for perfect normality, just no severe skew or outliers pulling in one direction.


## Step 3: Check assumptions — visually

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-align: center

ggplot(IOP, aes(x = d)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth = 0.1,
               fill = "#0072B2",
               color = "white",
               dotsize = 0.6) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  labs(title = "Differences: synthetic − adrenaline",
       x = "Difference in pressure reduction",
       y = NULL) + 
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0))
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

\

**What to look for:** approximate symmetry around zero — no severe skew, no
outliers pulling strongly in one direction.

\

**What we see:**

- Most differences are positive — the synthetic drug tends to reduce pressure more
- The spread on both sides of zero is roughly balanced
- With a small sample, we do not assume normality — only approximate symmetry

\

✓ Assumption 3 is satisfied.

:::
:::

## How ranking works — conceptually

\

```{r}
#| echo: false

# IOP %>%
#   filter(d != 0) %>%
#   mutate(
#     abs_d = abs(d),
#     rank  = rank(abs_d, ties.method = "average"),
#     signed_rank = if_else(d > 0, rank, -rank)
#   ) %>%
#   select(patient, adrenaline, synthetic, d, abs_d, rank, signed_rank) %>%
#   gt() %>%
#   cols_label(
#     patient = "Patient",
#     adrenaline = "Adrenaline",
#     synthetic = "Synthetic",
#     d = "d",
#     abs_d = "|d|",
#     rank = "Rank of |d|",
#     signed_rank = "Signed rank"
#   ) %>%
#   tab_options(table.font.size = 22)

# IOP %>%
#   filter(d != 0) %>%
#   mutate(
#     abs_d = abs(d),
#     rank  = rank(abs_d, ties.method = "average"),
#     signed_rank = if_else(d > 0, rank, -rank)
#   ) %>%
#   select(patient, adrenaline, synthetic, d, abs_d, rank, signed_rank) %>%
#   knitr::kable(col.names = c("Patient",
#     "Adrenaline",
#     "Synthetic",
#     "d",
#     "|d|",
#     "Rank of |d|",
#     "Signed rank"))

library(kableExtra)

IOP %>%
  filter(d != 0) %>%
  mutate(
    abs_d = abs(d),
    rank  = rank(abs_d, ties.method = "average"),
    signed_rank = if_else(d > 0, rank, -rank)
  ) %>%
  select(patient, adrenaline, synthetic, d, abs_d, rank, signed_rank) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Patient", "Adrenaline", "Synthetic", "d", 
                  "abs(d)", 
                  "Rank of abs(d)", 
                  "Signed rank"),
    align = "c"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(font_size = 28, full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(1, width = "5em") %>%
  column_spec(2:3, width = "7em") %>%
  column_spec(4:5, width = "4em") %>%
  column_spec(6, width = "7em") %>%
  column_spec(7, width = "7em", background = "#FFF3CD", bold = TRUE)

    
```

\

- Patient 5 had $d = 0$ → dropped from analysis (effective $n = 6$)
- $T^+ = 1.5 + 3 + 4.5 + 4.5 + 6 = 19.5$ (sum of positive ranks)
- $T^- = 1.5$ (sum of negative ranks, in absolute value)
- If $H_0$ true: $T^+$ and $T^-$ should be roughly equal


## Steps 4–5: Test statistic and p-value in R

\

```{r}
# exact = FALSE because ties exist in the nonzero differences
sr_result <- wilcox.test(IOP$d, mu = 0,
                         alternative = "two.sided",
                         exact = FALSE)

sr_result %>% 
  broom::tidy()
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
res_p <- lamisc::fmt_pvl(sr_result$p.value)
res_stat <- lamisc::fmt_num(sr_result$statistic, accuracy = 0.01)
res_diff <- scales::number(median(IOP$d), accuracy = 0.01)
```

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### Reading the output
- `statistic` = $T^+$ = `r res_stat` (sum of positive ranks)
- `p.value` = p-value using normal approximation — R uses the exact method only when sample is small **and** there are no ties. Because our nonzero differences contain ties (0.3 appears twice, 0.5 appears twice), R falls back to the normal approximation automatically. Setting `exact = FALSE` makes this explicit and prevents a warning.
:::

## Using `rstatix`

\

```{r}
library(rstatix)

IOP %>%
  wilcox_test(d ~ 1, mu = 0,
              alternative = "two.sided",
              exact = FALSE)
```

## Step 6: Conclusion


\

With *p* = `r res_p` and $\alpha = 0.05$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

\

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
### Conclusion

There is insufficient evidence that the median difference in intraocular pressure reduction between the synthetic drug and adrenaline differs from zero (Wilcoxon signed-rank test, $T^+$ = `r res_stat`, *p* = `r res_p`).

The synthetic drug showed a numerically higher median reduction (difference = `r res_diff` mmHg), but this difference was not statistically significant at $\alpha = 0.05$ with only 6 evaluable patients. A larger study would be needed to draw a definitive conclusion.
:::

\

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
### Note on sample size

$n = 7$ (effective $n = 6$) gives this study very low power. Failing to reject $H_0$ here does not mean the drugs are equivalent — it may simply mean we don't have enough data.
:::

# Part 3: Wilcoxon Rank-Sum Test

## The nonparametric two-sample t-test

\

When the two-sample t-test assumptions don't hold and the groups are **independent**, use the **Wilcoxon rank-sum test** (also called the Mann-Whitney U test).

\

**The idea:**

1. Combine both groups into one dataset
2. Rank all observations from 1 to $N$
3. Sum the ranks within each group
4. If the groups have the same median, the rank sums should be roughly equal
5. If one group tends to have higher ranks, that group has a higher median

\

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### Hypotheses

$H_0$: The distributions of the two populations are identical

$H_A$: The distributions of the two populations are not identical

(When both groups share the same shape, any difference reflects a shift in location — i.e., a difference in median.)
:::


## The rat neuron example

\

A study measured **evoked membrane current** of dental sensory neurons in rats under two conditions:

- **Control:** standard conditions
- **Treatment:** capsaicin + capsazepine (CPZ)

6 rats per group. Membrane current values (pA) are highly right-skewed with a wide range — a classic case for the rank-sum test.

\

**Question:** Does CPZ treatment alter the median membrane current?

```{r}
#| echo: false
CPZ <- tibble(
  group   = c(rep("Control", 6),
              rep("Cap + CPZ", 6)),
  current = c(3024, 2164, 864,
              780, 125, 110,
              426, 232, 130,
              94, 75, 55)
)
```


## Rat neuron data

\

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}

```{r}
CPZ
```

:::

::: {.column width="5%"}
:::

::: {.column width="55%"}

```{r}


CPZ %>%
  group_by(group) %>%
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    median = median(current),
    mean   = round(mean(current), 0)
  )
```

:::
:::::

## Visualizing the data

```{r}
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-align: center
#| code-fold: true

ggplot(CPZ, aes(x = current, fill = group, color = group)) +
  geom_dotplot(binwidth = 80, alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ group, ncol = 2, 
             scale = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("#E69F00", "#0072B2"), guide = "none") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#E69F00", "#0072B2"), guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Evoked membrane current by treatment group",
       x = "Membrane current (pA)", y = NULL) + 
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0))
```

Both groups are **right-skewed** with similar shape — consistent with the rank-sum assumption. The control group has a much higher median.

## How ranking works — conceptually

\

::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}
```{r}
CPZ %>%
  arrange(current) %>%
  mutate(rank = rank(current, 
                     ties.method = "average"))
```
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

\

\

\

\

\

\


- $W_{\text{Control}} = 4 + 5 + 9 + 10 + 11 + 12 = 51$
- $W_{\text{Cap+CPZ}} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 6 + 7 + 8 = 27$

\

- If $H_0$ true, we'd expect rank sums to be roughly equal (both ≈ 39).

:::
:::




## Step 1: State hypotheses

\

$H_0$: The distribution of evoked membrane current is the same in the control and CPZ-treated populations

$H_A$: The distributions of evoked membrane current in the control and CPZ-treated populations are not identical


## Steps 2–3: Significance level and assumptions

\

$\alpha = 0.05$

\

**Wilcoxon rank-sum assumptions:**

1. Two independent samples (✓ — different rats in each group)
2. Continuous outcome (✓ — membrane current in pA)
3. Both distributions have the **same shape** — check visually

The dotplots showed both groups are right-skewed with similar spread. ✓

## Steps 4–5: Test statistic and p-value in R

\
```{r}
# exact = TRUE is appropriate: both n < 10, no ties in this dataset
rs_result <- wilcox.test(current ~ group, data = CPZ,
                         alternative = "two.sided",
                         exact = TRUE)

rs_result %>%
  broom::tidy()
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
res_p   <- lamisc::fmt_pvl(rs_result$p.value)
res_stat <- lamisc::fmt_num(rs_result$statistic, accuracy = 0.01)
res_med_ctrl <- scales::number(median(CPZ$current[CPZ$group == "Control"]), accuracy = 1)
res_med_cpz  <- scales::number(median(CPZ$current[CPZ$group == "Cap + CPZ"]), accuracy = 1)
```

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### Reading the output
- `statistic` = $W$ = rank sum for the first alphabetical group
- `p.value` = exact p-value (appropriate here: both $n < 10$, no ties)
- For larger samples or when ties exist, R automatically uses the normal approximation
:::

## Using `rstatix`

\
```{r}
CPZ %>%
  wilcox_test(current ~ group,
              alternative = "two.sided",
              exact = TRUE)
```

## Step 6: Conclusion

\

With *p* = `r res_p` and $\alpha = 0.05$, we **fail to reject $H_0$**.

\

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
### Conclusion

<!-- "picoamps" (or more formally, "picoamperes"). -->

There is suggestive but inconclusive evidence that the distributions of evoked membrane current differ between the control group and the group exposed to capsaicin + CPZ (Wilcoxon rank-sum test, $W$ = `r res_stat`, *p* = `r res_p`).

The control group had a substantially higher median current (median = `r res_med_ctrl` pA) compared to the CPZ group (median = `r res_med_cpz` pA), but the small sample size ($n = 6$ per group) limits statistical power.
:::

# Part 4: Kruskal-Wallis Test

## The nonparametric ANOVA


When ANOVA assumptions fail with 3 or more groups, use the **Kruskal-Wallis test**.

\

It extends the rank-sum test to $k$ groups:

1. Combine all groups, rank all observations 1 to $N$
2. Compute the sum of ranks within each group: $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_k$
3. If $H_0$ is true, rank sums should be similar across groups
4. The test statistic $H$ quantifies how different the rank sums are


$$H_0: \text{The distributions of all } k \text{ groups are identical}$$
$$H_A: \text{At least one group has a different distribution}$$

\

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
The K-W test statistic $H$ follows a $\chi^2$ distribution with $k - 1$ degrees of freedom. When $k = 2$, K-W gives the same result as the rank-sum test.

When all groups share the same distributional shape, any difference reflects a shift in location — i.e., a difference in median.
:::

## Kruskal-Wallis vs ANOVA

\


![](/img_slides/Kruskal-Wallis-Test-vs-anova.png){fig-align="center" width="80%"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.6em; color: grey; text-align: center;"}
Source: numiqo Team (2026). numiqo: Online Statistics Calculator. https://numiqo.com
:::

## The IL-6 example

\

Serum **interleukin-6 (IL-6)** is an inflammatory cytokine commonly elevated in autoimmune and infectious disease. A researcher measured IL-6 levels (pg/mL) in patients classified into three disease severity groups.

- **Mild** ($n = 8$), **Moderate** ($n = 8$), **Severe** ($n = 8$)
- IL-6 values are highly right-skewed — a classic case for the Kruskal-Wallis test

<!-- picograms per milliliter -->

\

**Question:** Do IL-6 levels differ across disease severity groups?
```{r}
#| echo: false
set.seed(620)
il6_data <- tibble(
  severity = factor(
    c(rep("Mild", 8), rep("Moderate", 8), rep("Severe", 8)),
    levels = c("Mild", "Moderate", "Severe")
  ),
  il6 = c(
    round(rlnorm(8, meanlog = 1.5, sdlog = 0.4), 1),
    round(rlnorm(8, meanlog = 2.8, sdlog = 0.5), 1),
    round(rlnorm(8, meanlog = 4.0, sdlog = 0.6), 1)
  )
)
```

## IL-6 data

\

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
```{r}
il6_data
```

:::

::: {.column width="5%"}
:::

::: {.column width="55%"}
```{r}
il6_data %>%
  group_by(severity) %>%
  summarise(
    n      = n(),
    median = median(il6),
    mean   = mean(il6),
    sd     = sd(il6)
  )
```

:::
:::::

## Why not ANOVA?

\
```{r}
# Check the equal-variance assumption
il6_data %>%
  group_by(severity) %>%
  summarise(sd = sd(il6)) %>%
  summarise(ratio = max(sd) / min(sd))
```

\

SD ratio > 2 and groups are right-skewed → ANOVA assumptions violated.
Kruskal-Wallis is the right choice.

## Visualizing IL-6 by severity group

```{r}
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4.5
#| fig-align: center
#| code-fold: true

ggplot(il6_data, aes(x = severity, 
                     y = il6, 
                     fill = severity, 
                     color = severity)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.3, 
               outlier.shape = NA, 
               width = 0.5) +
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.5, width = 0.15, size = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("#0072B2", "#E69F00", "#D55E00"),
                    guide = "none") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0072B2", "#E69F00", "#D55E00"),
                     guide = "none") +
  labs(title = "Serum IL-6 by disease severity",
       x = NULL, y = "IL-6 (pg/mL)")
```

Right-skewed distributions across all groups. Median IL-6 appears to increase
with severity. Groups are clearly not normally distributed.

## Dotplot of IL-6 by severity group

```{r}
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 4
#| fig-align: center
#| code-fold: true

ggplot(data = il6_data, 
       aes(x = il6, 
           fill = severity, 
           color = severity)) + 
  geom_dotplot(binwidth = 10, 
               alpha = 0.80) + 
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("#0072B2", "#E69F00", "#D55E00"),
                    guide = "none") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0072B2", "#E69F00", "#D55E00"),
                     guide = "none") + 
  facet_wrap(~ severity) + 
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
  labs(title = "Serum IL-6 by disease severity",
       x = NULL, y = "IL-6 (pg/mL)")
```

Right-skewed distributions within each group — consistent with what the boxplots showed.

## Step 1: State hypotheses

\

$H_0$: The distributions of serum IL-6 are identical across all three severity groups

$H_A$: At least one severity group has a different distribution of serum IL-6

## Steps 2–3: Significance level and assumptions

\

$\alpha = 0.05$

\

**Kruskal-Wallis assumptions:**

1. Independent observations across groups (✓ — different patients in each group)
2. Continuous or ordinal outcome (✓ — IL-6 in pg/mL)
3. All groups come from populations with the **same distributional shape** — check visually

The previous plots suggest right-skewed distributions across all three groups. ✓

## Steps 4–5: Test statistic and p-value in R

\
```{r}
kw_result <- kruskal.test(il6 ~ severity, data = il6_data)

kw_result %>%
  broom::tidy()
```
```{r}
#| echo: false
res_p    <- lamisc::fmt_pvl(kw_result$p.value)
res_stat <- lamisc::fmt_num(kw_result$statistic, accuracy = 0.01)
res_df   <- kw_result$parameter
```

\

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
### Reading the output
- `statistic` = $H$ = Kruskal-Wallis test statistic
- `p.value` = p-value from $\chi^2$ distribution with df = $k - 1$ = `r res_df`
- `parameter` = degrees of freedom
:::

## Using `rstatix`

\
```{r}
il6_data %>%
  kruskal_test(il6 ~ severity)
```

## Step 6: Conclusion

\

\

With $H$ = `r res_stat`, df = `r res_df`, *p* = `r res_p`, we **reject $H_0$**.

\

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
### Conclusion

There is strong evidence that the distribution of serum IL-6 differs across at
least two disease severity groups (Kruskal-Wallis test, $H$ = `r res_stat`,
df = `r res_df`, *p* = `r res_p`).

Descriptively, median IL-6 increased with severity — mild
(median = `r scales::number(median(il6_data$il6[il6_data$severity == "Mild"]), accuracy = 0.1)` pg/mL),
moderate
(median = `r scales::number(median(il6_data$il6[il6_data$severity == "Moderate"]), accuracy = 0.1)` pg/mL),
and severe
(median = `r scales::number(median(il6_data$il6[il6_data$severity == "Severe"]), accuracy = 0.1)` pg/mL).
:::

## Post-hoc comparisons after K-W (1/2)

\

Just like ANOVA, a significant K-W test tells us *something* differs — not *which* 
pairs. Follow up with pairwise Wilcoxon rank-sum tests with a correction:
```{r}
pairwise.wilcox.test(il6_data$il6, il6_data$severity,
                     p.adjust.method = "bonferroni")
```

## Post-hoc comparisons after K-W (2/2)

\

**Alternative using `rstatix`:**

\
```{r}
il6_data %>%
  wilcox_test(il6 ~ severity,
              p.adjust.method = "bonferroni",
              paired = FALSE)
```

\

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
There is no nonparametric equivalent of Tukey HSD. Bonferroni correction is a straightforward choice — it controls the family-wise error rate across all pairwise comparisons. With only 3 pairs here, the penalty for using Bonferroni is small.
:::


# Wrap-Up

## Choosing the right nonparametric test

\

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

| Question | Test |
|---|---|
| Is the median different from a value? (single sample) | Wilcoxon signed-rank |
| Do paired measurements differ? | Wilcoxon signed-rank |
| Do two independent groups differ? | Wilcoxon rank-sum |
| Do 3+ independent groups differ? | Kruskal-Wallis |

\

**Key decision rule:**

1. Are parametric assumptions met? → Use parametric test (more power)
2. Small $n$, skewed data, outliers, ordinal outcome? → Use nonparametric test

:::

::: {.column width="5%"}
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

**R functions summary:**

```{r}
#| eval: false

# Signed-rank (paired / one-sample)
wilcox.test(data$d, mu = 0)

# Rank-sum (two independent groups)
wilcox.test(outcome ~ group, 
            data = data)

# Kruskal-Wallis (3+ groups)
kruskal.test(outcome ~ group, 
             data = data)

# Post-hoc after K-W
pairwise.wilcox.test(
  data$outcome, data$group,
  p.adjust.method = "bonferroni"
  )
```

:::
:::::

## Connecting to what's next

\

**Lesson 18 (Wednesday):** Correlation and Simple Linear Regression

- Both variables are continuous — we're no longer comparing groups
- Pearson correlation: the parametric approach
- Spearman correlation: the nonparametric analogue (ranks again!)
- Simple linear regression: predicting one variable from another

\

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
### The big picture

Nonparametric tests are a safety net — they work when parametric assumptions fail. But they're not magic. If assumptions are met, stick with t-tests and ANOVA. If they aren't, you now have the right tools to handle it.

The rank-based logic you saw today also underlies **Spearman correlation**, which we'll see on Wednesday — so this was good preparation.
:::

## References and Further Reading

\

**Article**

Nahm FS. [**Nonparametric statistical tests for the continuous data: the basic concept and the practical use**](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4754273/). *Korean Journal of Anesthesiology*. 2016;69(1):8–14. doi:10.4097/kjae.2016.69.1.8

- Accessible overview of when and why to use nonparametric tests, with practical examples drawn from medical research

\

**Web resources** (numiqo Team, 2026. numiqo: Online Statistics Calculator. https://numiqo.com)

- [Parametric vs. nonparametric tests](https://numiqo.com/tutorial/parametric-and-non-parametric-tests) — overview and decision guidance
- [Wilcoxon signed-rank test](https://numiqo.com/tutorial/wilcoxon-test) — paired and single-sample
- [Mann-Whitney U / Wilcoxon rank-sum test](https://numiqo.com/tutorial/mann-whitney-u-test) — two independent groups
- [Kruskal-Wallis test](https://numiqo.com/tutorial/kruskal-wallis-test) — three or more groups
